content.problemStatement:
This is the part when I say I don't want ya 

I'm stronger than I've been before 

This is the part when I break free 

'Cause I can't resist it no more

Chef has an array AAA of length NNN.

Let BBB denote the array obtained by removing any **non-empty** subset of **indices** from the array AAA.

The removal of subset of indices is said to be good if:


Sum of the subarray B[i,j]B[i, j]B[i,j] is divisible by 222 for all pairs of indices (1≤i≤j≤∣B∣)(1 \leq i \leq j \leq |B|)(1≤i≤j≤∣B∣).
Find the number of good removals. Since the answer can be huge, print it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.

**Note:**


The sum of an empty array is 000, which is divisible by 222.
B[i,j]B[i, j]B[i,j] denotes the subarray [Bi,Bi+1,…,Bj][B_i, B_{i+1}, \ldots, B_j][Bi​,Bi+1​,…,Bj​].

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of the array.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers, denoting the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, total number of good removals modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N, the length of the array. The next line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of good removals modulo 1000000007.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: evens=2, odds=1 → answer = 2^2 = 4.\nTest case 2: evens=1, odds=2 → answer = 2^1 = 2.

hidden.label: no removal allowed case (empty removal excluded)

solution.approach:
A removal is good iff after removing the chosen indices the remaining array contains only even numbers (an empty array also satisfies the condition). Therefore every odd element must be removed. Let E be the number of even elements in the original array. Any subset of the even positions may also be removed. The set of removed indices is uniquely defined by:
- all odd indices (mandatory),
- an arbitrary subset of the even indices.
Hence the number of possible removal sets is 2^E. The problem requires the removed set to be non‑empty. If the original array has no odd elements, the removal set could be empty (choose no even index), which must be excluded. Consequently:
if (oddCount == 0) answer = 2^E - 1 else answer = 2^E.
All computations are performed modulo MOD = 1 000 000 007. The exponentiation is done with binary exponentiation in O(log E) time, which is fast enough because the total N over all test cases is ≤2·10^5.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) in the worst case due to modular exponentiation per test case (log E ≤ 18 for N ≤ 2·10^5). Practically O(N) because exponentiation is cheap.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space per test case.
